summary: Gruber refines his earlier argument about Android vs. iOS developer ecosystems, clarifying that while Android has exclusive capabilities iOS can't match (home screen replacements, keyboard replacements, system-level integrations), iOS has a thriving ecosystem of polished, well-crafted third-party apps that simply don't exist on Android despite being technically possible there. He argues that market share alone doesn't explain developer support, and that the two platforms are developing fundamentally different economies — iOS resembling a retail market where users pay for quality apps, and Android trending toward a web-like ad-supported model. He pushes back against Fred Wilson's advice to prioritize Android development, arguing that equivalent market share doesn't mean equivalent audiences or purchasing behavior. Gruber sees the divergence as a microcosm of the broader Apple vs. Google cultural difference.
key_points: iOS's best apps could technically exist on Android but don't, while Android's best apps couldn't exist on iOS — yet Android isn't winning in both categories as it theoretically could

Market share numbers are misleading because they ignore non-phone devices (iPad, iPod Touch), exclude worldwide data, and treat all smartphone users as equivalent when their purchasing behavior differs dramatically
The iOS and Android app economies are developing along fundamentally different lines — iOS as a retail market, Android trending toward web-style ad-supported economics
Fred Wilson's advice to prioritize Android development only makes sense if you want eyeballs rather than direct sales revenue
Even Swype, Android's most popular exclusive app, monetizes through OEM licensing deals rather than Android Market sales, suggesting something is broken about Android's direct-to-consumer sales model
money_quotes: iOS's best apps could exist for Android but don't. Android's best apps couldn't exist for iPhone.
There is no Iconfactory or Tapbots for Android.

If all you want are eyeballs, go to where the most eyeballs are. But what if you don't merely want eyeballs, but rather want to sell your app directly to users? In that case, you should go to where the sales are, and that means iOS.

The differences between the iOS App Store and Android Market are a microcosm of the differences between Apple and Google. Apple is a retailer, a purveyor of well-crafted goods that people will line up to purchase. Google is an advertising company that builds popular services that command large audiences.

One problem with the web as a model for mobile economics is that the web economy doesn't seem to work for many product categories. The web economy works great for Google, not so much for anything that is intended to be sold.
The bigger flaw in Wilson's reasoning is that he seems to think that all smartphone users are equivalent.
Sensational headlines are one of the worst aspects of web-based news media.
For iOS, it's about emotional appeal — art, design, the ineffable.
key_insight: Market share and developer ecosystem health are fundamentally different metrics — iOS attracts craftsmanship-oriented developers who sell directly to users, while Android trends toward a web-like ad economy, and these two models may never converge.
